description:
You are working as an automation-workflow specialist.  
    In the log archive under “/home/user/logs” there is a CSV-style file called  
    “event_stream.txt”. It contains one log event per line, with the fields  
    “TIMESTAMP,LEVEL,EVENT_NAME”.

    Your assignment is to create a concise frequency report of the different  
    log LEVELs that appear in this file.

    Required end-state of the system
    =================================
    1. A new directory called “/home/user/output” must exist.  
       • If it already exists, reuse it; otherwise create it.

    2. Inside that directory, create a text file named  
       “/home/user/output/level_counts.log”.

    3. The file must list the number of occurrences of each distinct LEVEL  
       that appears in /home/user/logs/event_stream.txt, sorted from the
       highest count to the lowest count.

    4. The format **must be EXACTLY** (no leading/trailing spaces):
           LEVEL:COUNT
       One LEVEL per line.  Use capital letters for LEVEL exactly as they
       appear in the input file.  A colon (:) is the only separator;
       do not add spaces before or after the colon.

    5. No header or footer lines, no blank lines, and no other punctuation.
       The automated test will compare the file byte-for-byte.

    6. You must generate the report using only standard GNU/Linux
       command-line tools that are normally available in a minimal
       distribution (e.g., cut, sort, uniq, awk, sed, etc.).  A typical
       solution needs no more than 5-10 commands executed in the shell.

    7. In addition to creating the file, display its full contents to stdout
       so that a supervising user can see the result immediately.

    Constraints & tips
    ------------------
    • You do NOT have root access.  
    • You should not modify the original log file.  
    • Think in terms of pipes: isolate the LEVEL column, sort it, run a
      frequency count with “uniq -c”, reorder it numerically, then re-format
      it as “LEVEL:COUNT”.

    Verification
    ------------
    The automated grader will:
      a. Check that “/home/user/output/level_counts.log” exists.  
      b. Verify that its contents exactly match the required format and the
         correct counts, sorted from highest to lowest frequency.

    When you are done you should be able to run:

        cat /home/user/output/level_counts.log

    and see something like:

        INFO:5
        ERROR:3
        WARNING:2

    (The exact numbers depend only on the contents of the provided
     /home/user/logs/event_stream.txt file.)

    Proceed in the terminal; do NOT hard-code the answer—derive it from the
    file using the appropriate Unix text-processing commands.
truth:
<!--
        The file that already exists before the task starts.
    -->
    <initial_file path="/home/user/logs/event_stream.txt"><![CDATA[
2024-06-01 10:00:00,INFO,UserLogin
2024-06-01 10:00:02,ERROR,PaymentFailure
2024-06-01 10:00:05,INFO,PageView
2024-06-01 10:00:07,WARNING,CacheMiss
2024-06-01 10:00:10,INFO,PageView
2024-06-01 10:00:12,ERROR,PaymentFailure
2024-06-01 10:00:15,ERROR,DBTimeout
2024-06-01 10:00:18,INFO,UserLogout
2024-06-01 10:00:20,WARNING,CacheMiss
2024-06-01 10:00:22,INFO,UserLogin
    ]]></initial_file>

    <!--
        Expected output file after the agent finishes the assignment.
    -->
    <expected_file path="/home/user/output/level_counts.log"><![CDATA[
INFO:5
ERROR:3
WARNING:2
    ]]></expected_file>
